entries.title: Entering, Retiring, and Deportment in Lodge
entries.short_summary: This section outlines the proper procedures for entering and retiring from a Masonic Lodge, as well as general rules for deportment and conduct while the Lodge is open. It emphasizes courtesy, promptness, and respect for the Master and the harmony of the Lodge, prohibiting discussions of religion and partisan politics.
entries.full_summary:
This section outlines the proper procedures for entering and retiring from a Masonic Lodge, as well as general rules for deportment and conduct while the Lodge is open. It emphasizes courtesy, promptness, and respect for the Master and the harmony of the Lodge, prohibiting discussions of religion and partisan politics.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0014 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Order of Business

This section outlines the standard order of business for a Masonic Lodge after it has been opened, detailing the sequence of activities from reading minutes and committee reports to handling petitions, motions, accounts, and new business, concluding with Grand Lodge communications and closing the Lodge. It also includes rules for brothers desiring to speak, limiting debate, and determining the Lodge's sense on questions.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0014 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0045 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Brother A. B.

This section, addressed to a newly initiated Brother A. B., congratulates him on joining the ancient and honorable fraternity of Freemasonry. It outlines the fundamental duties of a Mason towards God, his neighbor, and himself, emphasizing reverence, acting upon the square, and avoiding intemperance. It also details civic responsibilities, proper outward demeanor, the importance of lodge attendance without neglecting vocations, and the caution against arguing with those who ridicule the Institution. The text encourages seeking further Masonic knowledge from well-informed brethren and stresses the importance of carefully vetting potential candidates. Finally, it charges the new Mason to keep the mysteries of the Institution sacred and inviolable.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0045 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0054 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Second Section

This section introduces the Fellow Craft candidate to the concepts of Operative and Speculative Masonry, defining each and emphasizing the moral and religious aspects of Speculative Masonry, including the contemplation of creation and homage to Deity. It also details the historical practice of ancient brethren working in both forms and consecrating the seventh day for rest and contemplation. The section concludes with a biblical description of the two pillars of brass, their dimensions, and features like chapiters, network, and pomegranates, as they would appear on the passage to the Middle Chamber.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0054 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0082 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Guarded by the Tiler’s Sword

The Tiler's Sword serves as a reminder for Masons to maintain vigilance and discretion in their thoughts, words, and actions, especially when in the presence of the uninitiated, upholding the Masonic virtues of silence and circumspection.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0082 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0112 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: First Circuit

This section describes a ritualistic recitation known as the "First Circuit," where various lodge officers, including the Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, Senior Deacon, Junior Deacon, and Chaplain, recite verses from Psalm 91. The verses emphasize divine protection, safety, and deliverance from harm for those who trust in the Most High.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0112 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0113 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Second Circuit

This section describes a 'Second Circuit' within a ritual, featuring a sequence of scriptural passages recited by various lodge officers: the Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, Senior Deacon, Junior Deacon, and Chaplain. The verses collectively emphasize themes of divine trust, protection, and guardianship.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0113 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0122 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Musical Selection

This section details the 'Musical Selection' portion of a Masonic gathering, which includes a Chaplain's prayer invoking divine blessing for the proceedings and for those about to assume leadership roles. Following the prayer, the Marshal delivers an address explaining the significance of observing the Festival of St. John the Evangelist. The Marshal emphasizes that Freemasonry's purpose extends beyond social enjoyment, focusing on duty, reconsecration through solemn promises, and the propagation of core principles. The discourse highlights Freemasonry's historical influence, its role in promoting liberty, equality, fraternity, law, and order, and its teachings on the Fatherhood of God, the Brotherhood of Man, and the importance of loving God and neighbor. It also stresses the Craft's commitment to elevating humanity, stimulating patriotism, encouraging liberal arts and sciences, promoting education, and upholding the dignity of labor, while remaining aloof from specific creeds.

The text explains the purpose of the Craft and announces the upcoming induction of chosen brethren into their respective offices to preside over the Lodge for the next twelve months.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0122 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0126 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Worshipful Master

This section details the installation ceremony of a Worshipful Master, beginning with the Master's assent to fifteen Ancient Charges and Regulations that outline moral conduct, civic duties, obedience to Masonic law, and lodge governance. The ceremony proceeds with the investiture of the Master with the collar and the presentation of key Masonic implements and documents: the Square, Compasses, Rule, Line, Book of Constitutions, Lodge Charter, Grand Lodge Regulations, By-Laws, and the Holy Writings. Each item is accompanied by its symbolic meaning and practical instructions for the Master's responsibilities in governing the Lodge.

This section describes a procedural sequence during a lodge installation, where Grand Honors are given, the Worshipful Master seats the Lodge, and an appropriate ode is sung, with an exception noted for public installations.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0126 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0128 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Senior Warden

This section details the installation ceremony of the Senior Warden, including the Marshal's presentation, the oath of office, and the charge delivered by the Installing Officer. The charge emphasizes the symbolic meaning of the Level, representing equality and the transient nature of worldly distinctions, and outlines the Senior Warden's duties, such as regular attendance and governing the Lodge in the Master's absence.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0128 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0149 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Charge to the Grand Chaplain

The charge to the Grand Chaplain outlines the sacred duties of the office, including leading devotional exercises at Grand Communications and performing functions at public ceremonies. It emphasizes that while Masonry is not a religion, it serves as 'religion’s handmaid,' promoting universal tolerance. The Grand Chaplain is expected to communicate doctrines of morality and virtue to the brethren, and is entrusted with the Holy Bible, the Great Light in Masonry.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0149 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0166 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Iii. Essentials in Conduct

This section outlines the fundamental principles of conduct for a Mason, emphasizing the necessity of obedience to the Moral Law, the Civil Law of the country, and the specific Masonic jurisdiction where the Mason resides. It also notes that a Mason remains amenable to that jurisdiction even if they are not a member of any particular Lodge.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0166 -->

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0198 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: The Senior & Junior Deacons

The Senior and Junior Deacons are lodge officers responsible for introducing visitors and candidates, attending the Master and Wardens, and acting as their proxies in active lodge duties and rites. The Master-elect and Senior Warden-elect are responsible for appointing their respective Deacons. The Senior Deacon specifically introduces guests, while the Junior Deacon receives candidates and assists with Masonic regalia storage.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0198 -->
practical_elements: Arrive before the Lodge meeting begins.
If late, clothe properly, inform the Tyler, and ask for admission.
The Tyler informs the Junior Deacon, who requests permission from the Master for late admission.
Upon admission, proceed West of the Altar, give the due guard and sign of the Degree, and quickly take a seat.
To retire, move West of the Altar, give the appropriate signs, and then leave.
To speak, rise, be recognized, give the due guard and sign, and address remarks to the Master.
Opening of the Lodge
Pledge of Allegiance to the Flag
Reading of the Minutes of the Last Stated and subsequent Called Communications for reference
Unfinished Business
Reports of Committees
Petitions and Applications
Motions and Resolutions
Accounts
New Business
Grand Lodge Communications
Reading of the Minutes of the meeting for correction
Closing the Lodge
Any brother desiring to speak in the Lodge shall rise and address the Master in a respectful manner, and with Masonic form.
No brother shall be permitted to speak more than twice on any one question, without consent of the Master.
Debate shall be courteous and pertinent to the subject.
On all questions submitted to the Lodge, its sense shall be ascertained by a show of hands or by ballot.
Implore God's aid in all laudable undertakings.
Act upon the square and do unto your neighbor as you wish them to do unto you.
Avoid all irregularity and intemperance.
Be a quiet and peaceful citizen, true to your government, and just to your country.
Patiently submit to legal authority and conform to the government of your country.
Be particularly careful to avoid censure or reproach in your outward demeanor.
Attend regular lodge meetings frequently.
Do not let Masonry interfere with necessary vocations.
Do not suffer zeal for the Institution to lead into argument with those who ridicule it.
Converse with well-informed brethren to improve in Masonic knowledge.
Do not recommend a person for initiation unless convinced they will conform to Masonic rules.
Keep sacred and inviolable the mysteries of the Institution.
Explanation of objects on the passage to the middle chamber.
Description of the two pillars of brass: eighteen cubits high, twelve cubits circumference, five cubit high chapiters, hollow, with network and pomegranates.
Ancient brethren wrought six days and rested on the seventh.
The Tiler's Sword is an implement that guards the entrance to the Lodge.
Recitation of specific scriptural verses from Psalm 91.
Involvement of multiple lodge officers: Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, Senior Deacon, Junior Deacon, and Chaplain.
The term "First Circuit" implies a structured sequence or movement within the ritual.
Recitation of specific scriptural verses by the Master: 'If thou but trust in the Lord, there shall no evil happen unto thee, neither shall any plague come nigh thy dwelling.'
Recitation of specific scriptural verses by the Senior Warden: 'For he shall give His angels charge concerning thee; to keep thee in all thy ways.'
Recitation of specific scriptural verses by the Junior Warden: 'They shall bear thee in their hands, that thou hurt not thy foot against a stone.'
Recitation of specific scriptural verses by the Senior Deacon: 'Behold He that keepeth Israel, shall neither slumber nor sleep.'
Recitation of specific scriptural verses by the Junior Deacon: 'The Lord himself is thy keeper; the Lord himself is thy defense upon thy right hand.'
Recitation of specific scriptural verses by the Chaplain: 'The Lord shall preserve thee from all evil; yea, it is even He that shall keep thy soul.'
The sequence of recitations is referred to as a 'Second Circuit'.
Marshal introduces the invocation of Deity before important undertakings.
Chaplain leads a prayer.
All present respond with 'So mote it be' after the prayer.
Marshal delivers an address on the Festival of St. John the Evangelist and Masonic principles.
Observation of the Festival of St. John the Evangelist for social enjoyment, cementing brotherly love, and consecration to duty.
Induction of brethren into their respective offices
Presiding in the Lodge for twelve months
The new Master faces the East.
The Installing Officer addresses the Ancient Charges and Regulations to the Master.
The Master signifies assent to each charge by answering "I do".
The Master is invested with the insignia of office (the collar is placed upon his shoulders).
The Square is presented with its teaching.
The Compasses are presented with their teaching.
The Rule is presented with its teaching.
The Line is presented with its teaching.
The Book of Constitutions is given in charge, with instruction to search and read it in the Lodge.
The Lodge Charter is given in charge, with instruction for careful preservation and transmission to the successor.
The Regulations of the Grand Lodge relating to Subordinate Lodges and the By-Laws for the government of the same are given in charge, with instruction for careful and punctual execution.
The Holy Writings are placed in the Master's hands as a Great Light in Masonry.
The Installing Officer calls up the brethren.
The Installing Officer takes the Master by the hand and seats him in the Oriental Chair.
The Installing Officer declares the Master installed.
Grand Honors are given (unless it is a public installation).
The Master seats the Lodge.
A suitable ode is sung.
The Marshal presents the Senior Warden-elect to the Installing Officer.
The Senior Warden-elect takes a solemn promise upon the honor of a Mason to serve the Lodge and comply with Grand Lodge laws and ancient Masonic usages.
The Senior Warden is invested with the badge of office.
The Installing Officer delivers a charge to the Senior Warden.
The Senior Warden is instructed to attend stated meetings regularly.
The Senior Warden is to govern the Lodge in the Master's absence and assist him in his presence.
The Senior Warden is conducted to his appointed station while a song is sung.
Investiture with the jewel of office.
Lead devotional exercises of Grand Communications.
Perform sacred functions of holy calling at public ceremonies.
Communicate doctrines of morality and virtue to brethren in the Grand Lodge.
Care for the Holy Bible, the Great Light in Masonry.
Obedience to the Moral Law.
Obedience to the Civil Law of the country.
Obedience to the Masonic jurisdiction where the Mason resides.
Amenability of the Mason to their Masonic jurisdiction, even if not a member of any Lodge.
Introduce visitors to the Lodge.
Attend on the Master and Wardens.
Act as proxies for the Master and Wardens in active Lodge duties.
Receive candidates into the different degrees of Masonry.
Engage in the immediate practice of Masonic rites.
Senior Deacon: Receive and introduce all guests of the Lodge, ensuring they feel welcomed and comfortable.
Junior Deacon: Receive all candidates of the Lodge, ensuring they feel welcomed and comfortable.
Junior Deacon: Assist in ensuring all Masonic regalia is properly stored and cared for.
entries.symbolic_meaning:
The phrase "acting upon the square" symbolically represents living a life of integrity, fairness, and moral rectitude in dealings with others, aligning with the Masonic principle of upright conduct.

Operative Masonry refers to the proper application of architectural rules for structure, figure, strength, beauty, proportion, and correspondence. Speculative Masonry teaches to subdue passions, act upon the square, maintain a good report, secrecy, and charity. It is interwoven with religion, obliging Masons to pay rational homage to Deity, contemplate the glorious works of creation, and adore the divine Creator. The seventh day of rest is consecrated for contemplation of creation and adoration of the Creator.

The Tiler's Sword symbolizes the constant need for Masons to be watchful and guarded in their conduct, particularly concerning Masonic secrets and practices when outside the Lodge or before those not initiated.

The recitation of Psalm 91 symbolizes the profound divine protection and unwavering faithfulness available to those who dwell under the defense of the Most High. It conveys a sense of security, assurance, and deliverance from all forms of peril, both seen and unseen, through trust in a higher power.

The recited verses symbolize the unwavering divine protection and care bestowed upon those who place their trust in the Lord. They convey a message of spiritual security, emphasizing the constant vigilance and guardianship of a higher power over the faithful.

The invocation of Deity symbolizes the spiritual foundation of all Masonic endeavors and the reliance on divine guidance. The Festival of St. John the Evangelist, while a social occasion, is consecrated to duty, representing the ongoing commitment to Masonic principles and the renewal of vows by those taking on leadership. The Marshal's address outlines the symbolic and practical influence of Freemasonry, emphasizing its role as a moral and educational force in society, promoting universal brotherhood and adherence to divine and moral law.

The induction of officers signifies the continuity of leadership, the structured operation of the Lodge, and the fulfillment of the Craft's purposes through chosen individuals.

The Square teaches to regulate actions by rule and line, harmonizing conduct with principles of morality and virtue. The Compasses teach to limit desires, enabling one to rise to eminence by merit, live respected, and die regretted. The Rule directs punctual observance of duty, pressing forward in virtue, and having eternity in view. The Line teaches moral rectitude, avoiding dissimulation, and directing steps towards immortality. The Holy Writings are presented as an inestimable gift from God, the Great Light in Masonry, guiding to truth, happiness, and the whole duty of man.

The Level symbolizes that all men are descended from the same stock, share the same nature and hope, and that despite necessary distinctions for subordination, all are brethren. It also represents death as the 'grand leveler of human greatness,' reducing all to the same state where only goodness distinguishes individuals.

The office of Grand Chaplain symbolizes the spiritual and moral foundation of Masonry. The Holy Bible, entrusted to the Chaplain, represents the Great Light and source of divine guidance. Masonry's role as 'religion's handmaid' signifies its supportive function in moral and spiritual development, distinct from being a religion itself, and underscores its spirit of universal tolerance.
entries.candidate_lesson:
Masons should always exhibit courtesy, propriety, and respect within the Lodge. Punctuality is valued, and if late, proper protocol must be followed. During meetings, Masons must maintain decorum, avoid private conversations, and address all remarks to the Master. Discussions on religion and partisan politics are prohibited to preserve the peace and harmony of the Lodge.

The candidate learns the structured and orderly process by which a Masonic Lodge conducts its affairs, emphasizing decorum, respect for the Master, and the proper procedures for discussion and decision-making within the fraternity. This reflects the importance of order and governance in Masonic practice.

The candidate is taught the foundational principles of Masonic conduct, encompassing moral, ethical, civic, and fraternal duties. Key lessons include reverence for God, justice and charity towards others, self-control, loyalty to one's country, discretion in public and private life, and the importance of preserving the integrity and reputation of the Craft through personal conduct and careful selection of new members. The inviolability of Masonic mysteries is also presented as a distinguishing characteristic.

The candidate learns the distinction between Operative and Speculative Masonry, understanding that modern Freemasonry is primarily Speculative, focusing on moral and spiritual development. They are taught the importance of self-governance, ethical conduct (acting upon the square, good report, secrecy, charity), and a deep reverence for the Creator and His creation, including the practice of setting aside time for rest and contemplation.

Masons are taught to exercise silence and circumspection in all their dealings, especially regarding Masonic matters, to protect the integrity of the Craft and its teachings from the uninitiated.

The candidate is taught the importance of faith and trust in the Almighty, understanding that adherence to virtuous principles brings divine protection and safety. It instills a lesson of spiritual fortitude and the assurance that one will be defended from life's adversities.

The candidate is taught the profound importance of faith and trust in the divine, assuring them of spiritual protection and guidance throughout their life's journey. It reinforces the belief that a higher power watches over and defends them from harm.

Candidates are taught the importance of invoking divine blessing in all undertakings. They learn that Freemasonry is a society founded on ancient traditions and profound principles, not merely social enjoyment. Key lessons include the Fatherhood of God, the Brotherhood of Man, the importance of loving God and one's neighbor, and the cultivation of virtues such as liberty, equality, fraternity, and adherence to law and order. The Craft encourages intellectual growth through liberal arts and sciences, promotes education, and instills a sense of duty towards God, fellow men, and country, aiming to make individuals better citizens.

The passage, addressed to 'good friends,' explains the fundamental purpose of the Masonic Craft and highlights the organized process of establishing leadership within the Lodge for a defined period, emphasizing order and continuity.

The Worshipful Master is charged with upholding moral law, being a peaceful and law-abiding citizen, respecting civil authorities, working diligently, living creditably, and acting honorably. They must venerate the Order's rulers, submit to Lodge resolutions, avoid quarrels and excess, be cautious, courteous, and faithful to the Lodge. The Master is to respect true brethren, discountenance imposters, promote the good of society, cultivate social virtues, and propagate Masonic knowledge. They must pay homage to the Grand Master, conform to Grand Lodge edicts, and preserve the Ancient Landmarks. Regular attendance at Grand Lodge committees and communications is required, along with ensuring proper Lodge formation, initiation, and visitor examination. The Master is taught to regulate actions by morality, limit desires, observe duty, pursue virtue, avoid dissimulation, and be guided by divine truth.

The Senior Warden is reminded that no station of eminence should make one forget the brotherhood of man, as all are equal in the eyes of a Mason and ultimately in death. The importance of duty, knowledge of Masonry, and attachment to the Lodge for the faithful discharge of trust are highlighted. The lesson also includes the transient nature of worldly distinctions and the ultimate equality brought by death.

The Grand Chaplain's charge teaches that Masonry, while not a religion, is deeply rooted in moral and virtuous principles, acting as a supportive framework for spiritual growth. It emphasizes the importance of universal tolerance, the communication of ethical doctrines, and the reverence for the Holy Bible as a source of light and guidance. It also highlights the steadfastness and perseverance expected in discharging duties.

A Mason is expected to uphold universal moral principles, abide by the laws of their nation, and adhere to the regulations of their local Masonic jurisdiction. These obligations are fundamental to being a Mason, irrespective of specific lodge membership.
tradition_notes: Courtesy dictates arriving before a Lodge meeting begins.
The process of informing the Tyler, Junior Deacon, and Master for late admission is a traditional protocol.
Addressing all remarks to the Master is a long-standing tradition in Lodge proceedings.
The outlined order of business represents a traditional and structured approach to conducting Lodge meetings, ensuring efficiency and adherence to established protocols.
The rules for speaking and debate reflect long-standing Masonic principles of decorum and respectful discourse within the Lodge.
Freemasonry is described as an ancient fraternity, having existed from time immemorial.
The Institution is founded on excellent rules and useful maxims inculcated in Masonic lectures.
The greatest and best of men in all ages have been encouragers and promoters of the art, extending privileges and patronizing assemblies.
Freemasonry is considered under two denominations: Operative and Speculative.
Ancient brethren wrought in both Operative and Speculative Masonry.
Modern Masons work in Speculative Masonry only.
Ancient brethren consecrated the seventh day as a day of rest, following God's creation pattern.

The use of scriptural passages, specifically Psalm 91, is a traditional element in Masonic ritual, often recited by officers to convey moral and spiritual lessons regarding divine providence and protection.
Freemasonry has a tradition of invoking a blessing of Deity before any great or important undertaking.
The Festival of St. John the Evangelist is a traditional observance in Freemasonry, consecrated to duty and promoting social enjoyment and brotherly love.
Freemasonry's origins are described as ancient, with its beginnings not recorded on written pages of history.

The practice of inducting officers to preside over a Lodge for a specific term (e.g., twelve months) is a traditional aspect of Masonic governance and ensures the continuous operation and leadership of the Lodge.
The Master must assent to Ancient Charges and Regulations which point out the duty of the Master of a Lodge.
The Master must hold in veneration the original rulers and patrons of the Order of Freemasonry and their regular successors.
The Master must submit to the awards and resolutions of brethren in Lodge convened, consistent with the constitutions of the Order.
The Master must respect genuine and true brethren and discountenance dissenters from the Ancient Landmarks and Constitutions of Masonry.
The Master must conform to every edict of the Grand Lodge or General Assembly of Masons that is not subversive of the principles and groundwork of Masonry.
It is admitted that it is not in the power of any man, or body of men, to make innovations in the Landmarks of Masonry.
It is admitted that no new Lodge can be formed without permission of the Grand Lodge.
It is admitted that no countenance ought to be given to any irregular Lodge, or to any person clandestinely initiated therein, as being contrary to the ancient charges of the Order.
It is admitted that no person can be regularly made a Freemason in, or admitted a member of, any regular Lodge, without previous notice, and due inquiry into his character.
It is agreed that no visitors shall be received into your Lodge without due examination, and producing proper vouchers of their having been initiated in a regular Lodge.
Masters have supported these regulations in all ages before.
The practice of giving Grand Honors and singing an ode is part of the lodge installation ceremony.
The Senior Warden's promise includes strictly complying with 'ancient Masonic usages'.
The investiture of a Grand Officer with a jewel of office.
The specific duties assigned to the Grand Chaplain, including leading devotional exercises.
The philosophical stance of Masonry as 'religion's handmaid,' promoting universal tolerance.
The Holy Bible being recognized and entrusted as the Great Light in Masonry.
The Master-elect and Senior Warden-elect shall appoint their respective Deacons.
caution_notes: Do not engage in private conversations while the Lodge is open.
Do not disrupt the business of the Lodge through any action.
Do not discuss religion or partisan politics in Lodge.
Brothers must address the Master respectfully and with Masonic form when speaking.
Speaking is limited to twice per question without the Master's consent.
Debate must be courteous and relevant to the subject.
Avoid all irregularity and intemperance which may impair your faculties or debase the dignity of your profession.
Do not countenance disloyalty or rebellion.
Be particularly careful to avoid censure or reproach in your outward demeanor.
Do not suffer your zeal for the Institution to lead you into argument with those who, through ignorance, may ridicule it.
Be particularly attentive not to recommend a person for initiation unless you are convinced that he will conform to our rules.
Masons must be particularly cautious and guarded in their thoughts, words, and actions when in the presence of the uninitiated.
Do not be concerned in plots and conspiracies against the government; patiently submit to law and authorities.
Avoid private piques and quarrels, and guard against intemperance and excess.
Discountenance imposters and all dissenters from the Ancient Landmarks and Constitutions of Masonry.
It is not in the power of any man or body of men to make innovations in the Landmarks of Masonry.
No new Lodge can be formed without Grand Lodge permission; do not countenance irregular Lodges or clandestinely initiated persons.
No person can be regularly made a Freemason or admitted to a regular Lodge without previous notice and due inquiry into character.
No visitors shall be received without due examination and proper vouchers of regular initiation.
The Lodge Charter should never be out of the Master's immediate control and must be duly transmitted to the successor.
Grand Honors are not given during a public installation.

entries.source_heading: Entering, Retiring, and Deportment in Lodge

entries.title: Prince Hall Masonry
entries.short_summary: This section details the origins and history of Prince Hall Masonry, beginning with Prince Hall, a free Black man who was made a Mason in 1783 by an Irish Military Lodge in Massachusetts. After being denied dispensation by the Grand Lodge of Massachusetts, Hall petitioned the Grand Lodge of England and received a charter for African Lodge No. 459 in 1795. This led to the establishment of the first Prince Hall Grand Lodge. For 200 years, Prince Hall Grand Lodges were largely unrecognized by mainstream Freemasonry, a situation attributed significantly to racism. However, recognition began in 1989 with the United Grand Lodge of England, and by 1998, 28 of 51 mainstream Grand Lodges were in fraternal accord with their Prince Hall counterparts. The Grand Lodge of Ohio recognized the Prince Hall Grand Lodge of Ohio in 1995, allowing for unrestricted visitation between their respective lodges.
entries.full_summary:
This section details the origins and history of Prince Hall Masonry, beginning with Prince Hall, a free Black man who was made a Mason in 1783 by an Irish Military Lodge in Massachusetts. After being denied dispensation by the Grand Lodge of Massachusetts, Hall petitioned the Grand Lodge of England and received a charter for African Lodge No. 459 in 1795. This led to the establishment of the first Prince Hall Grand Lodge. For 200 years, Prince Hall Grand Lodges were largely unrecognized by mainstream Freemasonry, a situation attributed significantly to racism. However, recognition began in 1989 with the United Grand Lodge of England, and by 1998, 28 of 51 mainstream Grand Lodges were in fraternal accord with their Prince Hall counterparts. The Grand Lodge of Ohio recognized the Prince Hall Grand Lodge of Ohio in 1995, allowing for unrestricted visitation between their respective lodges.

<!-- PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0224 -->
### Enrichment from Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide
Source section: Pronunciation Key

This section provides a pronunciation key for various words, many of which are common in Masonic ritual and discourse. It includes rules for indicating accented syllables and short vowel sounds, followed by an alphabetical list of words with their phonetic pronunciations.
<!-- /PDF_STAGE5:blue-lodge-ritual-reference-guide-2021:section-0224 -->
practical_elements: Prince Hall was made a Mason by a traveling Irish Military Lodge in Massachusetts in 1783.
Prince Hall sought to form a lodge but was denied dispensation by the Grand Lodge of Massachusetts.
Prince Hall petitioned the Grand Lodge of England for a charter.
African Lodge No. 459 received a charter from the Grand Lodge of England after 12 years.
Prince Hall Grand Lodges spread across the country.
Mainstream Grand Lodges and Prince Hall Grand Lodges were unrecognized for 200 years.
The United Grand Lodge of England extended recognition to the Prince Hall Grand Lodge of Massachusetts in 1989.
The Grand Lodge of Ohio recognized the Prince Hall Grand Lodge of Ohio at its 1995 Annual Communication.
Recognition allows members of recognized Grand Lodges to visit each other's lodges without restriction.
CAPITAL letters indicate accented syllables
a - short a as in the word bat
i - short i as in the word ill or is
e - short e as in the word ethic
(e) - barely audible short e
u - short u as in the word but
tradition_notes: The denial of dispensation to Prince Hall by the Grand Lodge of Massachusetts highlights historical racial barriers within Freemasonry.
The petitioning of the Grand Lodge of England for a charter established a direct lineage for Prince Hall Masonry.
The 200-year period of non-recognition for Prince Hall Grand Lodges is a significant historical aspect.
The eventual recognition by mainstream Grand Lodges, starting with the United Grand Lodge of England, marks a shift in Masonic relations.

entries.title: Its Covering
entries.short_summary: The section describes the symbolic 'covering' of the Masonic Lodge as a clouded canopy or starry-decked heaven, representing the ultimate destination for good Masons. This ascent is achieved through the aid of a theological ladder, identified as Jacob's Ladder, which has three principal rounds: Faith, Hope, and Charity. These virtues admonish Masons to have faith in God, hope in immortality, and charity to all mankind, with Charity being highlighted as the greatest virtue, extending beyond life into eternity.
entries.full_summary: The section describes the symbolic 'covering' of the Masonic Lodge as a clouded canopy or starry-decked heaven, representing the ultimate destination for good Masons. This ascent is achieved through the aid of a theological ladder, identified as Jacob's Ladder, which has three principal rounds: Faith, Hope, and Charity. These virtues admonish Masons to have faith in God, hope in immortality, and charity to all mankind, with Charity being highlighted as the greatest virtue, extending beyond life into eternity.
entries.symbolic_meaning: The 'clouded canopy or starry-decked heaven' symbolizes the celestial realm and the ultimate spiritual goal for Masons. Jacob's Ladder represents the means of spiritual ascent from earth to heaven, guided by theological virtues. The three rounds of Faith, Hope, and Charity are symbolic of the essential moral and spiritual principles Masons should cultivate. Charity is emphasized as transcending temporal existence, signifying its eternal importance.
entries.candidate_lesson: Candidates are taught to cultivate Faith in God, Hope in immortality, and Charity towards all humanity. The lesson emphasizes that Charity is the most enduring and significant of these virtues, extending beyond earthly life and serving as a guiding principle for eternal conduct.
tradition_notes: The concept of Jacob's Ladder is drawn from the biblical vision of Jacob ascending to heaven, a traditional symbol in Freemasonry for spiritual progress.
The emphasis on Faith, Hope, and Charity aligns with traditional Christian virtues, interpreted within a Masonic context.

entries.title: The Furniture of a Lodge
entries.short_summary: The furniture of a Masonic Lodge is defined as the Holy Bible, Square, and Compasses. The Holy Bible is dedicated to God as His invaluable gift to humanity. The Square is dedicated to the Master, serving as the proper Masonic emblem of his office. The Compasses are dedicated to the Craft, instructing members to use them to circumscribe their desires and keep their passions and prejudices within due bounds, especially in their interactions with fellow Masons. The text also alludes to a concept that extends beyond the grave into eternity.
entries.full_summary: The furniture of a Masonic Lodge is defined as the Holy Bible, Square, and Compasses. The Holy Bible is dedicated to God as His invaluable gift to humanity. The Square is dedicated to the Master, serving as the proper Masonic emblem of his office. The Compasses are dedicated to the Craft, instructing members to use them to circumscribe their desires and keep their passions and prejudices within due bounds, especially in their interactions with fellow Masons. The text also alludes to a concept that extends beyond the grave into eternity.
practical_elements: The furniture of a Lodge consists of the Holy Bible, Square, and Compasses.
The Square is the proper Masonic emblem of the Master's office.
The Compasses are used by the Craft to circumscribe their desires and keep passions and prejudices in due bounds.
entries.symbolic_meaning: The Holy Bible symbolizes God's inestimable gift to man, providing moral and spiritual guidance. The Square symbolizes the Master's authority and his role in ensuring rectitude and proper conduct within the Lodge. The Compasses symbolize the importance for every Mason to practice self-governance, moderating their desires, passions, and prejudices to maintain harmony and brotherly love. The mention of extending beyond the grave through eternity suggests themes of immortality, enduring principles, or the eternal nature of divine truth.
entries.candidate_lesson: Candidates learn that the Holy Bible is a divine gift, serving as a guide for life. The Square teaches them about the authority and responsibility of leadership, as embodied by the Master. The Compasses instruct them in the vital lesson of self-control, urging them to regulate their personal desires, passions, and prejudices to live harmoniously with all people, particularly their Masonic brethren. This lesson also implies that the principles taught have an eternal significance.
tradition_notes: The dedication of the Holy Bible to God, the Square to the Master, and the Compasses to the Craft is a traditional Masonic interpretation of these items as the essential furniture of a Lodge.
source_notes: Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide | The Furniture of a Lodge | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\1st_Edition_Blue_Lodge_Ritual_Reference_Guide_Digital_Edition.md#the-furniture-of-a-lodge | section 28

entries.title: Third Circuit
entries.short_summary: This section describes the 'Third Circuit' within a Masonic ritual, featuring a call and response among the Master, Senior Warden, and Junior Warden. Each officer recites a scriptural passage emphasizing the Lord's attentiveness to the cries and prayers of the poor and righteous, promising deliverance from troubles.
entries.full_summary: This section describes the 'Third Circuit' within a Masonic ritual, featuring a call and response among the Master, Senior Warden, and Junior Warden. Each officer recites a scriptural passage emphasizing the Lord's attentiveness to the cries and prayers of the poor and righteous, promising deliverance from troubles.
practical_elements: Call and response ritualistic exchange between the Master, Senior Warden, and Junior Warden.
Recitation of specific scriptural passages by each officer.
entries.symbolic_meaning: The exchange symbolizes divine providence, the Lord's constant attention to the righteous, and the assurance of salvation and deliverance from troubles for those who cry out to Him. It reinforces themes of faith, divine support, and the power of prayer.
entries.candidate_lesson: The candidate is taught about the importance of faith and prayer, understanding that the Lord hears the cries of the righteous and will deliver them from their troubles. It instills a sense of divine protection and the moral imperative to live righteously.
tradition_notes: This segment is a specific ritualistic 'circuit' involving the principal officers of the lodge.
The scriptural quotes are from Psalm 34.
source_notes: Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide | Third Circuit | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\1st_Edition_Blue_Lodge_Ritual_Reference_Guide_Digital_Edition.md#third-circuit | section 114

entries.title: Second Circuit
entries.short_summary: This section details the 'Second Circuit', a ritualistic sequence where various lodge officers recite scriptural verses emphasizing divine trust and protection. The Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, Senior Deacon, Junior Deacon, and Chaplain each deliver a line from scripture, collectively conveying a message of divine guardianship and preservation from evil.
entries.full_summary: This section details the 'Second Circuit', a ritualistic sequence where various lodge officers recite scriptural verses emphasizing divine trust and protection. The Master, Senior Warden, Junior Warden, Senior Deacon, Junior Deacon, and Chaplain each deliver a line from scripture, collectively conveying a message of divine guardianship and preservation from evil.
practical_elements: The Master recites a verse about trusting in the Lord for protection.
The Senior Warden recites a verse about angels being given charge to keep one in all ways.
The Junior Warden recites a verse about being borne in hands to prevent injury.
The Senior Deacon recites a verse about the Lord, the keeper of Israel, neither slumbering nor sleeping.
The Junior Deacon recites a verse about the Lord being one's keeper and defense.
The Chaplain recites a verse about the Lord preserving from all evil and keeping one's soul.
The sequence of recitations constitutes a 'Second Circuit' within the ritual.
entries.symbolic_meaning: The recited verses symbolize the omnipresent and unwavering divine protection afforded to those who place their trust in a higher power. They convey the assurance that a divine guardian watches over and preserves individuals from harm, both physical and spiritual, through constant vigilance and angelic charge.
entries.candidate_lesson: The candidate is taught the profound importance of faith and unwavering trust in the divine. This segment instills the lesson that with such trust comes the promise of divine protection, guidance, and preservation from evil, reinforcing a sense of security and spiritual guardianship.
source_notes: Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide | Second Circuit | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\1st_Edition_Blue_Lodge_Ritual_Reference_Guide_Digital_Edition.md#second-circuit | section 115

entries.title: Opening
entries.short_summary: The opening of the Lodge involves a formal processional entry, typically accompanied by music, after which all members take their seats before the formal exercises of the meeting begin.
entries.full_summary: The opening of the Lodge involves a formal processional entry, typically accompanied by music, after which all members take their seats before the formal exercises of the meeting begin.
practical_elements: Lodge members enter in procession
Music should be played during the procession
All members are to be seated
Exercises will be opened
tradition_notes: The tradition of a processional entry for the Lodge opening.
The customary use of music during the opening procession.

entries.title: Chaplain
entries.short_summary: This section outlines the sacred duties and responsibilities of the Chaplain within a Masonic Lodge, emphasizing their role in leading devotions when requested by the Worshipful Master. It clarifies that Freemasonry is not a religion and should not be seen as a substitute for the Church, yet its tenets are founded on the teachings of the Great Light in Masonry, recognizing the need for Divine guidance.
entries.full_summary: This section outlines the sacred duties and responsibilities of the Chaplain within a Masonic Lodge, emphasizing their role in leading devotions when requested by the Worshipful Master. It clarifies that Freemasonry is not a religion and should not be seen as a substitute for the Church, yet its tenets are founded on the teachings of the Great Light in Masonry, recognizing the need for Divine guidance.
practical_elements: The Chaplain's duty is to lead the Lodge in its devotions.
The Chaplain leads devotions when requested by the Worshipful Master.
An appropriate musical selection may be sung during the installation.
An address may be made after the musical selection.
A charge is rehearsed by the Installing Officer to the Chaplain.
entries.symbolic_meaning: The Chaplain symbolizes the spiritual dimension within the Lodge, representing the recognition of a Supreme Being and the need for Divine guidance among its members. The 'sacred trust' highlights the profound responsibility of fostering a reverent atmosphere while maintaining the distinct non-religious nature of Freemasonry.
entries.candidate_lesson: Candidates learn that while Freemasonry is not a religion, it is founded on moral and ethical principles derived from sacred texts like the Holy Bible, and it encourages a belief in a Supreme Being and the pursuit of spiritual and moral improvement. They are taught that the Lodge is not a substitute for their personal religious affiliations.
tradition_notes: The principle tenets of Freemasonry are based upon the teachings found in the Great Light in Masonry, the Holy Bible.
Freemasons, as religious men, recognize their need for Divine guidance.
caution_notes: Freemasonry never has been, is not now, nor will it ever be, a religion.
Members should never accept the Lodge as a substitute for the Church.
source_notes: Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide | Chaplain | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\1st_Edition_Blue_Lodge_Ritual_Reference_Guide_Digital_Edition.md#chaplain | section 136

entries.title: Worshipful Master
entries.short_summary: The Worshipful Master is charged with the superintendence and government of the Lodge's brethren, emphasizing the significant obligations and responsibilities inherent in their role as the head of the Lodge.
entries.full_summary: The Worshipful Master is charged with the superintendence and government of the Lodge's brethren, emphasizing the significant obligations and responsibilities inherent in their role as the head of the Lodge.
practical_elements: Superintendence of the brethren
Government of the brethren
Faithful discharge of important duties
entries.symbolic_meaning: The role of the Worshipful Master symbolizes leadership, authority, and the profound responsibility of governing a Masonic Lodge.
entries.candidate_lesson: The text highlights the high standards and significant responsibilities expected of the Worshipful Master, implicitly teaching all members about the importance of duty, leadership, and the proper governance within the fraternity.
tradition_notes: This describes the traditional and fundamental role and responsibilities of the Worshipful Master within a Masonic Lodge.

entries.title: Chaplain
entries.short_summary: This section outlines the role of the Chaplain, who is responsible for leading the Lodge in devotions when requested by the Worshipful Master. It emphasizes that Freemasonry is not a religion and should not be a substitute for the Church, yet its core principles are rooted in the teachings of the Great Light in Masonry, the Holy Bible, acknowledging the need for Divine guidance.
entries.full_summary: This section outlines the role of the Chaplain, who is responsible for leading the Lodge in devotions when requested by the Worshipful Master. It emphasizes that Freemasonry is not a religion and should not be a substitute for the Church, yet its core principles are rooted in the teachings of the Great Light in Masonry, the Holy Bible, acknowledging the need for Divine guidance.
practical_elements: The Chaplain's duty is to lead the Lodge in its devotions when requested by the Worshipful Master.
An appropriate musical selection may be sung after the installation charge.
An address may be made after the musical selection.
A charge is rehearsed by the Installing Officer after the address.
entries.symbolic_meaning: The Chaplain symbolizes the spiritual aspect acknowledged within Freemasonry, guiding members in recognizing the need for Divine guidance, even while maintaining that the Institution itself is not a religion. The Great Light in Masonry, the Holy Bible, serves as the foundation for its moral and ethical tenets.
entries.candidate_lesson: The Chaplain is entrusted with a sacred position to provide spiritual leadership. All members are taught that Freemasonry is not a religion and should not replace their individual religious affiliations, but it encourages a recognition of Divine guidance and bases its principles on the teachings of the Holy Bible.
tradition_notes: The principle tenets of Freemasonry are based upon the teachings found in the Great Light in Masonry, the Holy Bible.
As religious men, Masons recognize their need for Divine guidance.
caution_notes: Freemasonry never has been, is not now, nor will it ever be, a religion.
Its members should never accept the Lodge as a substitute for the Church.
source_notes: Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide | Chaplain | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\1st_Edition_Blue_Lodge_Ritual_Reference_Guide_Digital_Edition.md#chaplain | section 139

entries.title: Charge to the Grand Deacons
entries.short_summary: This section outlines the charge given to the newly appointed Grand Deacons of the Grand Lodge. It details their investment with the jewel of their office and rods as ensigns of authority. Their primary duties include attending upon the Grand Master and Grand Wardens, acting as their proxies in Grand Lodge duties, and performing their functions with vigilance and attention.
entries.full_summary: This section outlines the charge given to the newly appointed Grand Deacons of the Grand Lodge. It details their investment with the jewel of their office and rods as ensigns of authority. Their primary duties include attending upon the Grand Master and Grand Wardens, acting as their proxies in Grand Lodge duties, and performing their functions with vigilance and attention.
practical_elements: Appointment as Grand Deacons
Investment with the jewel of office
Investment with rods as ensigns of authority
Attending upon the Grand Master and Grand Wardens
Acting as proxies for the Grand Master and Grand Wardens in active Grand Lodge duties
entries.symbolic_meaning: The jewel of office and the rods serve as symbolic ensigns of the Grand Deacons' authority and the importance of their role within the Grand Lodge structure.
entries.candidate_lesson: The charge emphasizes the importance of diligence, vigilance, and attention in fulfilling one's duties, particularly in positions of authority and responsibility within the Masonic fraternity. It also highlights the structured hierarchy and the specific roles of officers within the Grand Lodge.
tradition_notes: The investment with a jewel and rods is a traditional ceremonial act for Grand Lodge officers, signifying their assumption of office and authority.
caution_notes: The instruction to 'Let vigilance and attention actuate you' serves as a caution against negligence or inattentiveness in the discharge of their important functions.
source_notes: Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide | Charge to the Grand Deacons | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\1st_Edition_Blue_Lodge_Ritual_Reference_Guide_Digital_Edition.md#charge-to-the-grand-deacons | section 153

entries.title: Charge to the Grand Sword Bearer
entries.short_summary: The Grand Sword Bearer is formally invested with the jewel of their office and charged with the duty of carrying the Sword of State in all public processions of the Grand Lodge.
entries.full_summary: The Grand Sword Bearer is formally invested with the jewel of their office and charged with the duty of carrying the Sword of State in all public processions of the Grand Lodge.
practical_elements: Investment with the jewel of office
Entrusted with the Sword of State
Duty to carry the Sword of State in all public processions of the Grand Lodge
entries.symbolic_meaning: The Sword of State is entrusted to the Grand Sword Bearer, signifying a specific duty and role within the Grand Lodge's public functions.
tradition_notes: The tradition of a Grand Sword Bearer carrying the Sword of State in Grand Lodge public processions.

entries.source_heading: Charge to the Grand Sword Bearer

entries.title: Charge to the Grand Pursuivant
entries.short_summary: This charge outlines the duties of the Grand Pursuivant, a Grand Lodge officer. Key responsibilities include receiving reports from the Grand Tiler, announcing the names and Masonic ranks of those seeking admission, ensuring all entrants wear appropriate decorations, and maintaining punctual attendance at all Grand Lodge Communications. The officer's station is near the door, and they are invested with the jewel of their office.
entries.full_summary: This charge outlines the duties of the Grand Pursuivant, a Grand Lodge officer. Key responsibilities include receiving reports from the Grand Tiler, announcing the names and Masonic ranks of those seeking admission, ensuring all entrants wear appropriate decorations, and maintaining punctual attendance at all Grand Lodge Communications. The officer's station is near the door, and they are invested with the jewel of their office.
practical_elements: Receive all reports from the Grand Tiler.
Announce the name and Masonic rank of all who desire admission.
Ensure none enter without wearing their appropriate decorations.
Maintain punctual attendance at all Communications of the Grand Lodge.
Stationed near the door.
Investiture with the jewel of office.
tradition_notes: The officer is invested with the jewel of their office.
The station of the Grand Pursuivant is traditionally near the door.
caution_notes: Ensure that none enter without wearing their appropriate decorations.

entries.title: Iv. Qualifications of Admission
entries.short_summary: This section outlines the fundamental qualifications required for admission into Freemasonry, which include being a man, of free birth, of lawful age, having a good reputation, and applying of one's own free will and consent.
entries.full_summary: This section outlines the fundamental qualifications required for admission into Freemasonry, which include being a man, of free birth, of lawful age, having a good reputation, and applying of one's own free will and consent.
practical_elements: Manhood
Free birth
Lawful age of maturity
Under the tongue of good report (good reputation)
Free will and consent
entries.candidate_lesson: A candidate learns the foundational requirements for joining the fraternity, emphasizing personal character, autonomy, and societal standing.
tradition_notes: These qualifications are traditional requirements for admission into a Blue Lodge.

entries.title: Vi. Government of the Craft
entries.short_summary: This section outlines the fundamental principles governing the Craft, detailing the requirements for regularly constituted Lodges, the hierarchy of appeals from Lodge decisions, and the specific prerogatives of a Grand Master. It also covers rules regarding inter-Lodge relations, entry into a Lodge, the indispensable secrecy of the ballot box, and the structure of Grand Lodges and General Assemblies.
entries.full_summary: This section outlines the fundamental principles governing the Craft, detailing the requirements for regularly constituted Lodges, the hierarchy of appeals from Lodge decisions, and the specific prerogatives of a Grand Master. It also covers rules regarding inter-Lodge relations, entry into a Lodge, the indispensable secrecy of the ballot box, and the structure of Grand Lodges and General Assemblies.
practical_elements: Lodges must be regularly constituted, duly tiled, properly furnished, and governed by a Master with the assistance of two Wardens.
Appeals from a Lodge Master's decision are made to the Grand Master, Grand Lodge, or General Assembly.
No Lodge can try its Master.
Every Mason has the right to appeal actions of his Lodge to the Grand Lodge or General Assembly.
No Lodge can interfere with the work of any other Lodge.
Entry into a Lodge requires personal knowledge, strict trial, due examination, or legal information.
The secrecy of the ballot box is indispensable.
The Grand Master presides over every meeting of the Craft within his jurisdiction.

The Grand Master has the prerogative to grant dispensation to confer degrees at irregular times, to temporarily set aside by-laws, to open and hold Lodges (dissolvable at his pleasure), and to make Masons at sight with the consent and assistance of Masons he assembles.
The Grand Master can assemble the Grand Lodge at his pleasure.
Grand Lodges, General Assemblies, or General Meetings govern Masons, and all Masons have a right to be present if the meeting is opened in the degree they have attained.
Members of Lodges are represented at Grand Lodge by their Master and Wardens, or their representatives, with the power and privilege to instruct them.
tradition_notes: Though elected by Grand Lodges, the office of Grand Master was not created by them, suggesting a traditional or inherent authority.
caution_notes: No Lodge can try its Master.
No Lodge can interfere with the work of any other Lodge.
None can enter a Lodge unless personally known, without a strict trial, due examination, or legal information.
Secrecy of the ballot box is indispensable.

entries.title: The Chaplain
entries.short_summary: The Chaplain holds a sacred, appointed position within the lodge, serving as its spiritual leader at the discretion of the Worshipful Master. While Freemasonry is not a religion and should not be a substitute for the Church, its core tenets are founded upon the teachings of the Great Light in Masonry, the Holy Bible. The Chaplain's duties include leading lodge devotions when requested and attending to the spiritual needs of the lodge and its members, acknowledging the need for Divine guidance.
entries.full_summary: The Chaplain holds a sacred, appointed position within the lodge, serving as its spiritual leader at the discretion of the Worshipful Master. While Freemasonry is not a religion and should not be a substitute for the Church, its core tenets are founded upon the teachings of the Great Light in Masonry, the Holy Bible. The Chaplain's duties include leading lodge devotions when requested and attending to the spiritual needs of the lodge and its members, acknowledging the need for Divine guidance.
practical_elements: The Chaplain is appointed at the will and pleasure of the Worshipful Master.
The Chaplain is installed as the spiritual leader of the lodge.
The Chaplain may be asked to lead the lodge in its devotions when requested by the Worshipful Master.
The Chaplain should serve the spiritual needs of the Lodge and its membership.
entries.symbolic_meaning: The Chaplain symbolizes the lodge's recognition of the need for Divine guidance and the spiritual foundation of Masonic tenets, even though Freemasonry itself is not a religion. The role represents the integration of moral and spiritual principles derived from sacred texts within the fraternity.
entries.candidate_lesson: Candidates learn that Freemasonry, while not a religion, acknowledges and emphasizes the importance of spiritual guidance and moral principles, often derived from sacred texts like the Holy Bible. They are taught that the Lodge is not a substitute for religious institutions, but rather complements a member's personal faith through the office of the Chaplain.
tradition_notes: The position of Chaplain is a sacred one within the lodge structure.
The Chaplain is appointed by the Worshipful Master, indicating a traditional method of selecting this officer.
caution_notes: Freemasonry has never been, is not now, nor will it ever be, a religion.
Members should never accept the Lodge as a substitute for the Church.
source_notes: Blue Lodge Ritual Reference Guide | The Chaplain | C:\Users\bar16\OneDrive\Documents\code-clean-start\PDF_handle\consolidated_books\1st_Edition_Blue_Lodge_Ritual_Reference_Guide_Digital_Edition.md#the-chaplain | section 200
